<!-- prettier-ignore-start -->

# TS9103

**Category:** Derive · **Severity:** Error

## Message

```
@deriving on union types requires a discriminant field
```

## Explanation

When deriving typeclasses for discriminated unions (sum types),
each variant must have a common discriminant field.

Example of correct usage:
  type Shape =
    | { kind: "circle"; radius: number }
    | { kind: "rect"; width: number; height: number };
                ^^^^
                Common discriminant field "kind"

Without a discriminant, the runtime cannot determine which variant to use.

<!-- prettier-ignore-end -->
<!-- generated:end -->

## Example

```typescript
/** @derive(Eq) */
// error: TS9103 — @deriving on union types requires a discriminant field
type Shape = { radius: number } | { width: number; height: number };
```

## How to fix

Give every variant the same literal-typed field, with a distinct value per variant:

```typescript
/** @derive(Eq) */
type Shape = { kind: "circle"; radius: number } | { kind: "rect"; width: number; height: number };

const a: Shape = { kind: "circle", radius: 1 };
const b: Shape = { kind: "circle", radius: 1 };
Shape.Eq.equals(a, b); // true — compares within the "circle" variant
```

The generated instance switches on the discriminant to know which variant it's looking at; without a shared literal field there is nothing to switch on at runtime. Any consistent field name works (`kind`, `type`, `_tag`, ...) as long as every variant carries it with a unique literal value.
